title: Compile and link model

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Compile and link model; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ translation preprocesses each translation unit, checks declarations and templates, emits object code, and links definitions under the one-definition rule.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A template definition remains visible in a header so a translation unit can instantiate it for int before linking. Your goal is to classify and repair preprocessing, compilation and linking failures. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor; Program structure and main. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ translation preprocesses each translation unit, checks declarations and templates, emits object code, and links definitions under the one-definition rule. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Templates and inline entities can appear in multiple units under ODR conditions, modules alter delivery, and link-time optimization can blur physical phase boundaries. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: If separate .cpp files compile, declarations and definitions across them cannot violate a C++ program rule.

includes: Relate source files, headers, object files, libraries and the One Definition Rule at an introductory level.
Classify and repair preprocessing, compilation and linking failures.
excludes: Templates and inline entities can appear in multiple units under ODR conditions, modules alter delivery, and link-time optimization can blur physical phase boundaries.

outcomes: Classify and repair preprocessing, compilation and linking failures.

examples.title: Compile and link model: defined case
examples.situation: A template definition remains visible in a header so a translation unit can instantiate it for int before linking.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A template definition remains visible in a header so a translation unit can instantiate it for int before linking.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Translation units compile independently, but linked entities must still satisfy compatible declarations and the one-definition rule across the entire program.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Translation units compile independently, but linked entities must still satisfy compatible declarations and the one-definition rule across the entire program.

examples.title: Compile and link model: boundary case
examples.situation: Two non-inline definitions of the same external function compile separately but violate the one-definition rule at link or beyond.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: Two non-inline definitions of the same external function compile separately but violate the one-definition rule at link or beyond.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Templates and inline entities can appear in multiple units under ODR conditions, modules alter delivery, and link-time optimization can blur physical phase boundaries.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: If separate .cpp files compile, declarations and definitions across them cannot violate a C++ program rule.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Classify and repair preprocessing, compilation and linking failures.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: If separate .cpp files compile, declarations and definitions across them cannot violate a C++ program rule.
misconceptions.correction: Translation units compile independently, but linked entities must still satisfy compatible declarations and the one-definition rule across the entire program.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: If separate .cpp files compile, declarations and definitions across them cannot violate a C++ program rule.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Compile and link model: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Templates and inline entities can appear in multiple units under ODR conditions, modules alter delivery, and link-time optimization can blur physical phase boundaries.

Keep this boundary in view: Templates and inline entities can appear in multiple units under ODR conditions, modules alter delivery, and link-time optimization can blur physical phase boundaries.
